title: Today's Tech Brief: S&P 500 blocks fast SpaceX entry, won’t waive rule for unprofitable AI firms
summary: A brief look at the tech headlines, including SpaceX's stock market entry and a hair growth product review.
imageAlt: Image from source article for S&P 500 blocks fast SpaceX entry, won’t waive rule for unprofitable AI firms
imageCaption: S&P 500 blocks fast SpaceX entry, won’t waive rule for unprofitable AI firms
imageSourceTitle: S&P 500 blocks fast SpaceX entry, won’t waive rule for unprofitable AI firms

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. We're looking at a couple of interesting stories from the world of tech. First up, SpaceX has been trying to get into the S&P 500, but it's being blocked by a rule that prevents unprofitable companies from joining the index. Now, I know what you're thinking - isn't SpaceX a hugely successful company? And you're right, it is. But the thing is, it's still not profitable, at least not yet. And that means it doesn't meet the S&P's requirements.

The S&P 500 has a rule that says you need to have four consecutive quarters of profitability to be considered for entry into the index. It's a pretty standard rule, but it's causing some issues for companies like SpaceX that are investing heavily in research and development. My read is that this rule is in place to protect investors from companies that might not be financially stable. But it's also worth noting that some of these unprofitable companies might be working on some really innovative and important technologies.

S&P 500 blocks fast SpaceX entry, won’t waive rule for unprofitable AI firms is also worth a quick mention. I was 30 years old, and it seemed like a logical choice given the slow thinning of my always-thin hair and the pleasantly round shape of my head, the one physical trait I consistently rece... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
